title: Lesson 4: Growing Deep Roots in Jesus
description: The name of this course is Growing Deep, and this is the lesson where the name comes to life. Jesus is not only your good Shepherd, your good Healer, and your good Savior. He is also your Good Soil. The spiritual disciplines are the roots that go down into Him for the rest of your life, and the deeper those roots go, the more of His fruit shows up above the surface.

blocks.text: You have met the Father who runs toward you, the Son who gave you a new identity, and the Spirit who walks with you as your Guide. That is the Trinity arc of the first three lessons. Now you step into the heart of this course. The name Growing Deep is not a slogan. It is the shape of a life with Jesus. Before a plant grows up, it grows down. Before fruit ever shows on the branches, roots are quietly reaching into the soil. This lesson is about the roots.

blocks.alt: A farmer scattering seed across four patches of ground, with one flourishing plant in rich soil whose deep roots are visible beneath the surface

blocks.title: The Sower and the Soil
blocks.text:
Jesus sat in a small boat just off the shore, and the people crowded the beach to hear Him. He told them a story about a farmer and a handful of seed.

"Listen! Behold, a sower went out to sow. And as he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it. Other seed fell on rocky ground, where it did not have much soil, and immediately it sprang up, since it had no depth of soil. And when the sun rose, it was scorched, and since it had no root, it withered away. Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked it, and it yielded no grain. And other seeds fell into good soil and produced grain, growing up and increasing and yielding thirtyfold and sixtyfold and a hundredfold." And he said, "He who has ears to hear, let him hear." (Mark 4:3-9)

Four soils. Same seed. Same sun. Same rain. The difference was the ground. The hard path never let the seed in. The rocky ground had no depth, so the plant sprang up fast and died faster, because there was no root. The thorny ground let the weeds win. Only the good soil let the roots go down, and the harvest was staggering.

Most people read this parable looking in a mirror, and that is a good place to start. Which soil am I right now? Is my heart hard, shallow, crowded, or open? That question is always worth asking. But there is a deeper reading of this parable that Christians have treasured for centuries, and it is the door into this whole lesson.
Jesus Is the Good Soil

You already know some of the names of Jesus. He is the good Shepherd who finds the one lost sheep and carries it home (John 10:11). He is the good Healer who touches the leper, opens blind eyes, and makes broken bodies whole (Mark 1:41). He is your good Savior who took your sin on the cross and rose again to give you life (1 Timothy 1:15). There is one more name worth holding onto from this parable. Jesus is your Good Soil.

Where your heart was path-hard, rocky-shallow, or thorn-choked, Jesus was the one honest and good heart that heard God's Word perfectly, trusted it fully, obeyed it completely, and produced the hundredfold harvest that you never could. And because you are now in Him, His soil becomes your soil. Your roots do not have to find their own way in hard ground. They grow down into the Good Soil that Jesus already is. The deeper they go, the more of His life you receive.
Therefore, as you received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk in him, rooted and built up in him and established in the faith, just as you were taught, abounding in thanksgiving.

Paul picks his words carefully. Rooted in Him. Built up in Him. Established in Him. The Christian life is not a better version of you working harder. It is the same you sinking roots into the same Jesus, a little deeper every season. Just as you received Him, so you keep walking in Him.
Growing Deep Before You Grow Tall

The world measures a tree by what it sees above the ground. God measures it by what is happening underneath. A plant whose roots sit near the surface looks fine until the heat comes. Then it withers. A tree whose roots have patiently sunk down into the water table stands through droughts, storms, and summers that flatten everything else. This is the Bible's picture of a person who knows Jesus.

His delight is in the law of the Lord, and on his law he meditates day and night. He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither. In all that he does, he prospers.

Blessed is the man who trusts in the Lord, whose trust is the Lord. He is like a tree planted by water, that sends out its roots by the stream, and does not fear when heat comes, for its leaves remain green, and is not anxious in the year of drought, for it does not cease to bear fruit.

Notice what these two passages share. A tree. A stream. Roots reaching down to the water. Fruit that shows up in season. No anxious striving. The root work is slow, hidden, and unglamorous, and most of the world will never see it. But it is the reason the tree is still standing when the wind blows through.

4.1 - Read Jesus' parable in Mark 4 again slowly. Which soil best describes your heart in this season of life, and which soil do you most want to grow toward? What does that tell you about where you need to go deeper in Jesus?
Five Roots That Go Down Into Jesus

Spiritual disciplines are simply the roots. They are not extra credit, and they are not how you earn God's favor. They are the slow, ordinary practices that keep drawing your life up out of Jesus, the Good Soil. Five of them show up across the whole Bible, and together they form the root system of a life with Jesus. Walk the tool below and watch each root reach down into the soil, one at a time.

These five roots will return throughout the rest of this course and the courses that follow. You will take a full lesson on prayer, another on the Bible, another on the church, another on giving, and another on fighting sin and temptation. Think of this lesson as the tree trunk that holds all of those together. Every single one of these disciplines has the same aim: not to make you more disciplined, but to sink your roots further into Jesus so that His life runs through you into everything you touch.
The Deepest Root: Day and Night in the Word

If you had to pick one root to focus on first, pick this one. The Bible is God's own voice to you, and the person whose roots are in Scripture is the person who stays green in a dry year. The goal is not a program or a method. The goal is a life that is quietly soaked in God's Word, day and night, the way a tree is soaked by an unseen stream.

This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth, but you shall meditate on it day and night, so that you may be careful to do according to all that is written in it. For then you will make your way prosperous, and then you will have good success.

There are four simple ways the Bible invites you to take in God's Word, and they build on each other like layers of a root system. First, you read. You open the Bible and let it talk to you, one passage at a time. A small amount each day, done faithfully, will change you more than a giant amount done once in a while. Second, you study. You slow down on a passage and ask what it actually says, who wrote it, who it was written to, and what it reveals about God and about you. Study is how reading grows teeth. Third, you memorize. You tuck a verse or two into your heart so that they are with you in the car, in the hospital waiting room, at two in the morning when sleep will not come. Memorized Scripture is the Word with you when no Bible is open. Fourth, you meditate. You chew on a verse slowly, the way you would chew a rich meal, turning it over in your mind and heart until it starts to work on you. The Psalms call this meditating day and night.

How can a young man keep his way pure? By guarding it according to your word. With my whole heart I seek you; let me not wander from your commandments! I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you.

Do not let the four words pressure you. Most days will only be reading, and that is enough. Some days a verse will rise off the page and you will want to study it, memorize it, or carry it around in your head all afternoon. Let the Word set the pace. The point is simply to be in it, day after day, because day after day is how roots grow.

4.2 - Of the four ways to take in Scripture - reading, studying, memorizing, and meditating - which one feels most natural right now, and which one does God seem to be inviting you into for this season? What is one small step you could take this week?
The Fruit Comes From the Roots

Here is the invitation and the comfort of this whole lesson. You do not have to manufacture your own fruit. You do not have to strain for peace, squeeze out joy, force patience, or white-knuckle your way into love. The fruit shows up on the branches when the roots are drinking from the soil. Jesus said it like this in the upper room:

Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it abides in the vine, neither can you, unless you abide in me. I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.

Abide is a quiet word. It means to stay, to remain, to keep living where you already are. Your roots in Jesus are not something you have to invent. You have been grafted into the Vine. The job is to stay there, to keep drinking, to keep going deeper. The fruit is His to grow, in His time, in the measure He chooses. Thirty, sixty, a hundredfold. That is not your effort producing results. That is the Good Soil doing what only He can do.

Character is the unseen part of a Christian's life. Most of the world will never know how much time you spent praying, how many verses you hid in your heart, how often you stayed faithful in a quiet hour no one noticed. But the fruit will show. A steadiness under pressure. A kindness that outlasts a bad day. A love for people who are hard to love. A slow, real peace that your old life never held. That is the Good Soil speaking through your branches. That is the kind of life Jesus is growing in you, one season at a time, for the rest of your life.

4.3 - Looking back at the five roots in the widget, which one is your strongest right now and which one is your shallowest? If you were going to deepen one root over the next month, which would it be, and what is the first small practice you would build into your week?
Lesson 4 Review

Jesus is your good Shepherd, good Healer, good Savior, and Good Soil. The parable of the sower teaches more than a warning about hard hearts. It is an invitation into the only soil that holds forever. The spiritual disciplines are the roots that sink down into Him. The deeper they go, the more of His life rises into the fruit above. Growing Deep is the whole point of this course, and it is the shape of a life with Jesus from today until the day you see Him.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
